nativeRange.habitat: Coastal rivers, streams, estuaries, and mangrove areas; tolerates a wide salinity range from fresh to full marine

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, hornwort, water sprite)

diet.notes: Mollies require a substantial plant matter component in their diet alongside protein sources. They accept flakes, pellets, and frozen foods readily, and benefit noticeably from algae wafers, spirulina flakes, and blanched vegetables such as spinach and zucchini. Live and frozen foods such as brine shrimp and daphnia are also readily taken.

avoidWith: cherry shrimp and small dwarf shrimp (fry risk)

imageCandidates.notes: James-LaFontaine (via iNaturalist), 2048x1063. Tack-sharp, vibrant sailfin nuptial male showing iridescent blue-green flanks, orange-edged caudal fin, and the large black-spotted sailfin dorsal display — diagnostic molly color/fin features on a fully colored live fish.
Marrabbio, ~1024px. Pair of Black Mollies with lyretail fins in a planted tank — the iconic all-black hobby morph (listed in alsoKnownAs), in-tank and showing the purple-black iridescence.

Cybularny, 5299x4149. Group of silver/black/gold mollies in a labeled planted shop tank ('molinezja silver'), representing the shoaling habit and color-form variety; individual fish are a bit small in frame so kept as non-recommended.

summary: The Molly is a freshwater livebearer widely considered hardy and easy to keep, available in a broad range of color forms, generally advised to be kept in hard, alkaline water. Prolific breeders well suited to beginners, Mollies have a notable appetite for vegetable matter alongside standard aquarium foods.
careNotes: Mollies are generally advised to be kept in hard, alkaline water (gH 15–30, kH 8–20, pH 7.5–8.5). Soft or acidic water has been associated with kidney problems and shortened lifespan, particularly in mass-produced fancy varieties; in areas where tap water runs soft, mineral supplementation is commonly recommended to bring hardness up to the preferred range. Maintaining at least a 2:1 to 3:1 female-to-male ratio helps reduce male harassment of females. Many commercially available Mollies are hybrids of P. sphenops and P. latipinna, which accounts for the wide variety of color forms sold in the hobby — Black, Gold, Dalmatian, Silver, Lyretail, Creamsicle, and Marble among them. The Sailfin Molly (Poecilia latipinna, with males reaching around 15 cm) is a distinct species that requires a longer tank (at least roughly 120 cm and 150 L) to give the male room to display his extended dorsal fin. Black Molly and Sailfin morphs have been reported as more sensitive to water quality fluctuations than standard P. sphenops. Mollies tend to be active nibblers, and slow-moving or long-finned tankmates have been known to suffer fin damage as a result. Fry are born live and are comparatively large, but adults will readily eat them without dense plant cover providing places to hide. Two related trade forms are worth knowing about: the Balloon Molly is a selectively bred morph of P. sphenops with a compressed body shape, a curved spine, and reduced swimming ability — a form considered ethically contentious by some keepers. The Liberty Molly (Poecilia salvatoris) is a distinct wild species from El Salvador, recognizable by its striking red-and-black fin coloration, but its care requirements — hard, alkaline water, a similar diet, and compatible tankmates — are essentially the same as for the common Molly.
breedingNotes: Mollies are highly prolific livebearers, with gestation typically running 4–8 weeks and females producing 20–100 fry per brood at roughly monthly intervals. Females are capable of storing sperm and producing multiple broods from a single mating. No parental care is provided, and adults will readily eat their own fry; dense plant growth — Java moss, water sprite, and floating plants — substantially improves fry survival by giving young fish places to hide. Fry are large enough at birth to accept crushed flake food, infusoria, and powdered fry food immediately. Separating a visibly gravid female into a dedicated breeding tank or breeding box before she gives birth is a commonly used method for maximising the number of fry that survive.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source for care parameters, tank sizing, diet, and temperament.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Poecilia sphenops
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, native range, water parameters (temp 21-28°C, pH 7.0-8.5, hardness 15-30 dH), adult size (males 8 cm, females 12 cm). Temperature and hardness ranges align with key data.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Poecilia latipinna
additional.notes: Cross-reference for Sailfin Molly variant data: size to 12.5 cm male, 11 cm female; temp 21-26°C; pH 7.0-8.5; hardness 15-35 dH. Confirms euryhaline nature and herbivorous diet emphasis.

additional.name: EasyClean Aquatics — Molly Fish
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (7.5-10 cm), lifespan (3-5 yrs), breeding (20-100 fry per brood), escape risk (jumping confirmed), min tank 20 gal / 75L. Hardness cited as 10-25 dGH (slightly lower than SeriouslyFish; kept gH min at 15 per playbook averaging with key data).

fish.breedingNotes: Mollies are prolific livebearers that produce 20–100 fry roughly monthly with no specialized setup beyond a female-to-male ratio of at least 2:1 to 3:1 and dense planting to give fry places to hide from adults.
